candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture distinguish genuinely load-bearing parts from merely historically entrenched survivals when the problem-history is incomplete, distorted, or inaccessible? The core tension is whether genealogical reconstruction can reliably track explanatory necessity, or whether it risks confusing contingent historical selection with real counterfactual dependence.

What is the relation between origin-constraints and present explanatory structure: can a component be load-bearing now even if it was not introduced to solve the original anomaly, or cease to be load-bearing despite its historical role? This tests whether explanatory necessity is fundamentally historical, structural, or some evolving combination of both.

How should the conjecture handle explanations that were not produced through a single criticism-driven lineage but through recombination, abstraction, or mathematical reformulation across multiple traditions? The open question is whether 'problem-history' scales to plural, branching, or merged genealogies without losing the ability to identify essential versus swappable components.
